Abstract Malware, also known as malicious code, refers to a program that is covertly inserted into another program with the intent to destroy data, run destructive or intrusive programs, or otherwise compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the victim’s data, applications, or operating system. Malware is the most common external threat to most hosts, causing widespread damage and disruption and necessitating extensive recovery efforts within most organizations. This publication provides recommendations for improving an organization’s malware incident prevention measures. It also gives extensive recommendations for enhancing an organization’s existing incident response capability so that it is better prepared to handle malware incidents, particularly widespread ones. Keywords incident response; information security; malware Acknowledgments The authors, Murugiah Souppaya of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and Karen Scarfone of Scarfone Cybersecurity, thank their colleagues who assisted with developing this revision of the publication. Acknowledgments, Original Version of SP 800-83 The authors, Peter Mell of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and Karen Kent and Joseph Nusbaum of Booz Allen Hamilton, wish to thank their colleagues who reviewed drafts of this document and contributed to its technical content. The authors would particularly like to acknowledge Tim Grance and Murugiah Souppaya of NIST and Lucinda Gagliano, Thomas Goff, and Pius Uzamere of Booz Allen Hamilton for their keen and insightful assistance throughout the development of the document. The authors would also like to express their thanks to security experts Mike Danseglio (Microsoft), Kurt Dillard (Microsoft), Michael Gerdes (Getronics RedSiren Security Solutions), Peter Szor (Symantec), Miles Tracy (U.S. Federal Reserve System), and Lenny Zeltser (Gemini Systems LLC), as well as representatives from the General Accounting Office, and for their particularly valuable comments and suggestions. The National Institute of Standards and Technology would also like to express its appreciation and thanks to the Department of Homeland Security for its sponsorship and support of NIST Special Publication 800- 83. NIST Incident Response Life Cycle in Cybersecurity | EC-Council Understanding the Incident Response Life Cycle March 30, 2022 | Incident Handling Incident response management is an integral part of cybersecurity operations. Incident responders are the first to react to any security incident: They help organizations identify, contain, eradicate, and recover from the incident. Incident handlers help create incident management plans for detection and recovery procedures. Incident handlers—and the entire company—can use these plans in the event of a cyberattack. This article will cover what you need to know about the incident response life cycle and how to help businesses prevent, or manage the aftermath of, a cyberattack. What Is the Incident Response Life Cycle? The incident response life cycle is a series of procedures executed in the event of a security incident. These steps define the workflow for the overall incident response process. Each stage entails a specific set of actions that an organization should complete. The Five Phases of the Incident Response Life Cycle There are several ways to define the incident response life cycle. The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST; Cichonski et al., 2012) developed a framework for incident handling, which is the most commonly used model. The process outlined in the NIST framework includes five phases: Preparation Detection and analysis Containment Eradication and recovery Post-event activity 1. Preparation In this phase, the business creates an incident management plan that can detect an incident in the organization’s environment. The preparation step involves, for example, identifying different malware attacks and determining what their impact on systems would be. It also involves ensuring that an organization has the tools to respond to an incident and the appropriate security measures in place to stop an incident from happening in the first place. 2. Detection and Analysis An incident response analyst is responsible for collecting and analyzing data to find any clues to help identify the source of an attack. In this step, analysts identify the nature of the attack and its impact on systems. The business and the security professionals it works with utilize the tools and indicators of compromise (IOCs) that have been developed to track the attacked systems. 3. Containment, Eradication, and Recovery This is the main phase of security incident response, in which the responders take action to stop any further damage. This phase encompasses three steps: Containment. In this step, all possible methods are used to prevent the spread of malware or viruses. Actions might include disconnecting systems from networks, quarantining infected systems (Landesman, 2021), or blocking traffic to and from known malicious IP addresses. Eradication. After containing the security issue in question, the malicious code or software needs to be eradicated from the environment. This might involve using antivirus tools or manual removal techniques (Williams, 2022). It will also include ensuring that all security software is up to date in order to prevent any future incidents. Recovery. After eliminating the malware, restoring all systems to their pre-incident state is essential (Mazzoli, 2021). This might involve restoring data from backups, rebuilding infected systems, and re-enabling disabled accounts. Explore Incident Response With ECIH Certification Post-Event Activity The final phase of the incident response life cycle is to perform a postmortem of the entire incident (Cynet, 2022). This helps the organization understand how the incident took place and what it can do to prevent such incidents from happening in the future. The lessons learned during this phase can improve the organization’s incident security protocols and make its security strategy more robust and effective. How to Become Ultimate Cybersecurity Incident Handler with ECIH - YouTube How to Become Ultimate Cybersecurity Incident Handler with ECIH EC-Council Watch on Tips for Improving an Incident Response Plan There are many ways to improve an organization’s incident management plan (HIMSS, 2022). Identify and train incident handlers in case there is a security breach. Ensure that all employees know their responsibilities when such an event occurs. These responsibilities may vary, but they will likely involve when to report an issue, who to contact, and what tools to immediately deploy in the event of a breach. Create effective communication channels across teams, ensuring that each person reports to their assigned contact. This helps ensure quick detection and recovery from any incidents in real time without losing much valuable information or data. Maintain logs for each system and update them regularly, leaving no gaps in the data. The creation of such logs can be useful in identifying the source of a security breach and preventing similar events in the future. Regularly test the incident response plan so that the documentation stays up to date with any changes made to security policies or new technologies introduced to the organization’s infrastructure. Become a Certified Incident Handler Prevent Security Incidents with an Incident Handler Certification At the end of the day, businesses need to ensure that they have the appropriate resources on hand to prevent a security breach from occurring and to know how to handle it if one does. EC-Council’s Certified Incident Handler (ECIH) certification program teaches cybersecurity professionals the skills they need to prepare for such an event and trains them to detect, analyze, and prepare for any security-related incident within an organization. Having ECIH-certified personnel on hand can benefit businesses in numerous ways, including reducing damages, increasing response times to security breaches, and greatly improving security posture. Interested in learning more? Visit the ECIH program page for information on the course, and contact EC-Council today to learn how to get certified. CrowdStrike SeverityMeaningHarris Health ResponseAcknowledge WithinEscalate To
Critical Active, confirmed threat with high impact — ransomware, active C2, credential theft, lateral movement Immediate containment. Phone call to L2 + SOC Manager. CISO notification for ransomware/breach. < 5 min L2 + SOC Mgr + CISO (if ransomware/breach)
High Strong indicators of malicious activity — unblocked malware, suspicious execution chain, defense evasion Immediate triage. Contain if threat is active. Always escalate to L2. < 10 min L2
Medium Suspicious activity requiring investigation — may be malicious or may be a false positive Triage within the shift. Investigate process tree and context. Escalate to L2 if unsure. < 30 min L2 if unsure
Low Minor policy violation or low-confidence detection — often benign but worth recording Triage when queue permits. Check against known FP list. Document and close. < 2 hours No escalation (unless pattern of multiple Lows on one host)
Informational Telemetry event — no threat indicated, but logged for forensic/audit purposes No action required unless it correlates with other alerts on the same host. No SLA No escalation
Override Rule: If a CrowdStrike detection involves a PHI system , domain controller , internet-facing server , or medical device , add +1 severity level to the response regardless of CrowdStrike's rating. Harris Health System — Security Operations Center v1.0 — Mar 2026 Sections 🏠 Overview 🖥️ Console Navigation 🔔 Reading a Detection 🌳 Process Tree Investigation 🔒 Host Containment & Lift 🎯 IOC Management 🔍 Hash & IOC Lookup 💻 Real-Time Response 🚫 Common False Positives Cheat Sheets 📖 Detection Names Decoder ⚔️ MITRE ATT&CK Decoder 🚦 Severity → SLA Mapping ⌨️ Keyboard Shortcuts CrowdStrike Falcon — SOC L1 Standard Operating Procedure This is the complete L1 analyst guide for CrowdStrike Falcon, Harris Health's primary EDR platform . It covers ~13,000 IT endpoints across 2 hospitals and 32 clinics. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint runs in passive mode alongside Falcon (for Purview telemetry only). CrowdStrike Overwatch provides 24/7 human-led threat hunting on top of automated detections. Console Login: falcon.crowdstrike.com (https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) — Use your Harris Health SSO credentials. If locked out, contact your SOC Manager. What Harris Health Uses CrowdStrike Falcon For
ModulePurpose at Harris HealthStatus
Falcon PreventNext-gen antivirus — blocks known malware, exploits, and ransomware at the endpointActive
Falcon Insight XDREndpoint detection and response — behavioral analytics, process tree visibility, IOC matchingActive
Falcon Overwatch24/7 human-led threat hunting — proactive alerts from CrowdStrike's elite threat huntersActive (Phase 0)
Falcon Intelligence (CAO)Threat intel, dark web monitoring for Harris Health, adversary tracking, healthcare threat profilesActive (Phase 0)
IOC ManagementCustom indicator blocking/detection — IPs, domains, file hashes pushed from TIP or manuallyActive
Real-Time Response (RTR)Remote shell to endpoints for live forensics, file retrieval, process kill, script executionActive
Network ContainmentIsolate a compromised endpoint from the network while maintaining CrowdStrike cloud connectivityActive
Key Architecture Note: CrowdStrike Falcon is the primary EDR. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint runs in passive mode alongside it (for Purview DLP telemetry only, not for active protection). If you see a Defender alert, it's informational — CrowdStrike is the blocking/containment tool. A 6-9 month parallel evaluation is underway to make a data-driven decision on whether to keep both. 🖥️ Console Navigation Use this section to find your way around the Falcon console. The console is accessed via falcon.crowdstrike.com (https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) using Harris Health SSO. Key Areas You'll Use Daily 1 Activity Dashboard (Home) Where: Landing page after login, or left menu → Activity → Detections What it shows: All active detections across the environment, sorted by severity and time. This is your primary alert queue for CrowdStrike alerts. L1 Action: Start here every shift. Filter by severity (Critical/High first), then review unassigned detections. 2 Host Management Where: Left menu → Host setup and management → Host Management What it shows: All enrolled endpoints — hostname, IP, OS, sensor version, containment status, last seen time. L1 Action: Search for a specific host by hostname or IP. Check if a host is online, contained, or in Reduced Functionality Mode (RFM). This is where you initiate Network Containment . 3 Threat Intelligence → IOC Management Where: Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management What it shows: All custom IOCs (IPs, domains, file hashes) that have been added — either from the TIP automatically or manually by analysts. L1 Action: Add new IOCs from threat advisories, search for existing IOCs, check if an indicator is already blocked. 4 Threat Intelligence → Intelligence Where: Left menu → Threat Intelligence → Intelligence What it shows: CrowdStrike's threat intelligence database — adversary profiles, malware families, vulnerability intelligence, IOC reputation lookups.

. If it shows "Containment Pending," the endpoint may be offline. 4 Verify containment Refresh the host page. Confirm status shows Contained . The host can still communicate with CrowdStrike cloud (so you can still run RTR commands and pull data) but cannot reach anything else on the network. Before containing a medical device: NEVER contain an endpoint on a medical device VLAN without Clinical Engineering approval. Medical devices may be actively involved in patient care. Use Cisco FMC microsegmentation as a compensating control instead. See the Medical Device playbook in the Triage Decision Tree. How to Lift Containment 1 Navigate to the contained host In Host Management , filter by "Contained" status at the top. Click the host name. 2 Click "Lift Containment" Click the "Status: Contained" button. Add a comment explaining why containment is being lifted (e.g., "Reimaged and verified clean by L2 — ticket INC0012345"). Click Confirm . 3 Verify normal status Refresh the page. Status should change from "Lift Containment Pending" to "Normal" . The host regains full network access. L1 Authority: L1 analysts CAN contain a host without prior approval during an active threat (malware executing, C2 callback, ransomware indicators). Document your reasoning in the alert notes and ServiceNow ticket. L1 analysts should get L2 approval before lifting containment to ensure remediation is complete. 🎯 IOC Management Use this section to add, search, and manage custom Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) in CrowdStrike Falcon. IOCs are pushed here from the TIP (Sentinel) automatically, but you may also need to add them manually from threat advisories. How to Add a Custom IOC 1 Navigate to IOC Management Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management . You'll see the list of all existing IOCs. 2 Click "Add IOC" Select the IOC type: File Hash (SHA256 preferred, also SHA1 or MD5), Domain , or IP Address . 3 Enter the IOC value For hashes, paste the full SHA256. For domains, enter without protocol (e.g., evil-domain.com , not https://evil-domain.com ). For IPs, enter the IPv4 address. 4 Set the action Detect — alerts only, does not block. Use when you want to monitor. Detect & Prevent — blocks execution/connection AND alerts. Use for confirmed malicious IOCs and active exploitation. 5 Set severity and description Match the advisory severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low). In the description, include the source: e.g., "Health-ISAC Advisory 2026-042" or "CISA KEV — CVE-2026-12345". 6 Set expiration Harris Health policy: no permanent IOCs . Set 30 days for actively exploited threats (renew if still active). Set 90 days for general threat intel. IOCs auto-expire and are cleaned up. 7 Save and verify Click Save . Verify the IOC appears in the IOC Management list. Monitor the Detection Summary for any hits. Searching for existing IOCs: Use the search bar at the top of IOC Management. Paste a hash, IP, or domain to check if it's already in the system before adding a duplicate. 🔍 Hash & IOC Lookup When you encounter an unknown file hash, IP, or domain during an investigation, use these tools to determine if it's malicious. Lookup Priority Order 1 CrowdStrike Intelligence (first) Where: Threat Intelligence → Intelligence → paste the hash, IP, or domain in the search bar. Why first: CrowdStrike's intel database includes healthcare-specific threat data and correlates with adversary campaigns. It's the most contextually relevant source for Harris Health. What to look for: Malware family, associated adversary group, kill chain phase, severity rating, and any related IOCs. 2 VirusTotal (second) Where: virustotal.com (https://www.virustotal.com) → paste the hash, IP, or domain. Why second: VirusTotal aggregates results from 70+ antivirus engines. Good for confirmation and broad coverage, but lacks the adversary context that CrowdStrike provides. What to look for: Detection ratio (e.g., 45/70 engines detect = confirmed malicious). Community comments. Behavioral analysis results if the file was sandboxed. OPSEC Warning: Do NOT upload actual files from Harris Health endpoints to VirusTotal. VirusTotal is a public service — uploaded files become visible to other researchers and potentially adversaries. Only submit hashes (SHA256) for lookup, never the actual file. If you need to detonate a file in a sandbox, use CrowdStrike's sandbox or coordinate with L2. 💻 Real-Time Response (RTR) Real-Time Response gives you a remote shell to a live endpoint. Use it for live forensics — pulling files, checking running processes, killing malicious processes. The endpoint must be online and have an active CrowdStrike sensor. Caution:

How to Lift Containment 1 Navigate to the contained host In Host Management , filter by "Contained" status at the top. Click the host name. 2 Click "Lift Containment" Click the "Status: Contained" button. Add a comment explaining why containment is being lifted (e.g., "Reimaged and verified clean by L2 — ticket INC0012345"). Click Confirm . 3 Verify normal status Refresh the page. Status should change from "Lift Containment Pending" to "Normal" . The host regains full network access. L1 Authority: L1 analysts CAN contain a host without prior approval during an active threat (malware executing, C2 callback, ransomware indicators). Document your reasoning in the alert notes and ServiceNow ticket. L1 analysts should get L2 approval before lifting containment to ensure remediation is complete. 🎯 IOC Management Use this section to add, search, and manage custom Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) in CrowdStrike Falcon. IOCs are pushed here from the TIP (Sentinel) automatically, but you may also need to add them manually from threat advisories. How to Add a Custom IOC 1 Navigate to IOC Management Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management . You'll see the list of all existing IOCs. 2 Click "Add IOC" Select the IOC type: File Hash (SHA256 preferred, also SHA1 or MD5), Domain , or IP Address . 3 Enter the IOC value For hashes, paste the full SHA256. For domains, enter without protocol (e.g., evil-domain.com , not https://evil-domain.com ). For IPs, enter the IPv4 address. 4 Set the action Detect — alerts only, does not block. Use when you want to monitor. Detect & Prevent — blocks execution/connection AND alerts. Use for confirmed malicious IOCs and active exploitation. 5 Set severity and description Match the advisory severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low). In the description, include the source: e.g., "Health-ISAC Advisory 2026-042" or "CISA KEV — CVE-2026-12345". 6 Set expiration Harris Health policy: no permanent IOCs . Set 30 days for actively exploited threats (renew if still active). Set 90 days for general threat intel. IOCs auto-expire and are cleaned up. 7 Save and verify Click Save . Verify the IOC appears in the IOC Management list. Monitor the Detection Summary for any hits. Searching for existing IOCs: Use the search bar at the top of IOC Management. Paste a hash, IP, or domain to check if it's already in the system before adding a duplicate. 🔍 Hash & IOC Lookup When you encounter an unknown file hash, IP, or domain during an investigation, use these tools to determine if it's malicious. Lookup Priority Order 1 CrowdStrike Intelligence (first) Where: Threat Intelligence → Intelligence → paste the hash, IP, or domain in the search bar. Why first: CrowdStrike's intel database includes healthcare-specific threat data and correlates with adversary campaigns. It's the most contextually relevant source for Harris Health. What to look for: Malware family, associated adversary group, kill chain phase, severity rating, and any related IOCs. 2 VirusTotal (second) Where: virustotal.com (https://www.virustotal.com) → paste the hash, IP, or domain. Why second: VirusTotal aggregates results from 70+ antivirus engines. Good for confirmation and broad coverage, but lacks the adversary context that CrowdStrike provides. What to look for: Detection ratio (e.g., 45/70 engines detect = confirmed malicious). Community comments. Behavioral analysis results if the file was sandboxed. OPSEC Warning: Do NOT upload actual files from Harris Health endpoints to VirusTotal. VirusTotal is a public service — uploaded files become visible to other researchers and potentially adversaries. Only submit hashes (SHA256) for lookup, never the actual file. If you need to detonate a file in a sandbox, use CrowdStrike's sandbox or coordinate with L2. 💻 Real-Time Response (RTR) Real-Time Response gives you a remote shell to a live endpoint. Use it for live forensics — pulling files, checking running processes, killing malicious processes. The endpoint must be online and have an active CrowdStrike sensor. Caution:

Harris Health System — Security Operations Center v1.0 — Mar 2026 Sections 🏠 Overview 🖥️ Console Navigation 🔔 Reading a Detection 🌳 Process Tree Investigation 🔒 Host Containment & Lift 🎯 IOC Management 🔍 Hash & IOC Lookup 💻 Real-Time Response 🚫 Common False Positives Cheat Sheets 📖 Detection Names Decoder ⚔️ MITRE ATT&CK Decoder 🚦 Severity → SLA Mapping ⌨️ Keyboard Shortcuts CrowdStrike Falcon — SOC L1 Standard Operating Procedure This is the complete L1 analyst guide for CrowdStrike Falcon, Harris Health's primary EDR platform . It covers ~13,000 IT endpoints across 2 hospitals and 32 clinics. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint runs in passive mode alongside Falcon (for Purview telemetry only). CrowdStrike Overwatch provides 24/7 human-led threat hunting on top of automated detections. Console Login: falcon.crowdstrike.com (https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) — Use your Harris Health SSO credentials. If locked out, contact your SOC Manager. What Harris Health Uses CrowdStrike Falcon For
ModulePurpose at Harris HealthStatus
Falcon PreventNext-gen antivirus — blocks known malware, exploits, and ransomware at the endpointActive
Falcon Insight XDREndpoint detection and response — behavioral analytics, process tree visibility, IOC matchingActive
Falcon Overwatch24/7 human-led threat hunting — proactive alerts from CrowdStrike's elite threat huntersActive (Phase 0)
Falcon Intelligence (CAO)Threat intel, dark web monitoring for Harris Health, adversary tracking, healthcare threat profilesActive (Phase 0)
IOC ManagementCustom indicator blocking/detection — IPs, domains, file hashes pushed from TIP or manuallyActive
Real-Time Response (RTR)Remote shell to endpoints for live forensics, file retrieval, process kill, script executionActive
Network ContainmentIsolate a compromised endpoint from the network while maintaining CrowdStrike cloud connectivityActive
Key Architecture Note: CrowdStrike Falcon is the primary EDR. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint runs in passive mode alongside it (for Purview DLP telemetry only, not for active protection). If you see a Defender alert, it's informational — CrowdStrike is the blocking/containment tool. A 6-9 month parallel evaluation is underway to make a data-driven decision on whether to keep both. 🖥️ Console Navigation Use this section to find your way around the Falcon console. The console is accessed via falcon.crowdstrike.com (https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) using Harris Health SSO. Key Areas You'll Use Daily 1 Activity Dashboard (Home) Where: Landing page after login, or left menu → Activity → Detections What it shows: All active detections across the environment, sorted by severity and time. This is your primary alert queue for CrowdStrike alerts. L1 Action: Start here every shift. Filter by severity (Critical/High first), then review unassigned detections. 2 Host Management Where: Left menu → Host setup and management → Host Management What it shows: All enrolled endpoints — hostname, IP, OS, sensor version, containment status, last seen time. L1 Action: Search for a specific host by hostname or IP. Check if a host is online, contained, or in Reduced Functionality Mode (RFM). This is where you initiate Network Containment . 3 Threat Intelligence → IOC Management Where: Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management What it shows: All custom IOCs (IPs, domains, file hashes) that have been added — either from the TIP automatically or manually by analysts. L1 Action: Add new IOCs from threat advisories, search for existing IOCs, check if an indicator is already blocked. 4 Threat Intelligence → Intelligence Where: Left menu → Threat Intelligence → Intelligence What it shows: CrowdStrike's threat intelligence database — adversary profiles, malware families, vulnerability intelligence, IOC reputation lookups.

🚦 CrowdStrike Severity → Harris Health SLA Mapping How CrowdStrike's 5-level severity scale maps to Harris Health's response expectations and SLAs.
CrowdStrike SeverityMeaningHarris Health ResponseAcknowledge WithinEscalate To
Critical Active, confirmed threat with high impact — ransomware, active C2, credential theft, lateral movement Immediate containment. Phone call to L2 + SOC Manager. CISO notification for ransomware/breach. < 5 min L2 + SOC Mgr + CISO (if ransomware/breach)
High Strong indicators of malicious activity — unblocked malware, suspicious execution chain, defense evasion Immediate triage. Contain if threat is active. Always escalate to L2. < 10 min L2
Medium Suspicious activity requiring investigation — may be malicious or may be a false positive Triage within the shift. Investigate process tree and context. Escalate to L2 if unsure. < 30 min L2 if unsure
Low Minor policy violation or low-confidence detection — often benign but worth recording Triage when queue permits. Check against known FP list. Document and close. < 2 hours No escalation (unless pattern of multiple Lows on one host)
Informational Telemetry event — no threat indicated, but logged for forensic/audit purposes No action required unless it correlates with other alerts on the same host. No SLA No escalation
Override Rule: If a CrowdStrike detection involves a PHI system , domain controller , internet-facing server , or medical device , add +1 severity level to the response regardless of CrowdStrike's rating. A "Medium" on a domain controller is treated as High. ⌨️ Keyboard Shortcuts Speed up your workflow in the Falcon console with these shortcuts. These work in most modern browsers (Chrome/Edge) within the Falcon web app.
ShortcutActionWhere
/Focus the global search barAny page — quickly search for hosts, IOCs, detections
Ctrl + KQuick navigation / command palette (if enabled)Any page — jump to any section fast
J / KNavigate up/down through detection listDetections queue — cycle through alerts without mouse
EnterOpen the selected detectionDetections queue — open alert detail
EscClose detail panel / go backAny detail view — return to the list
Ctrl + CCopy selected text (hash, IP, hostname)Detection detail — copy IOCs for lookup
Ctrl + FFind on pageAny page — search for specific text in long tables
Ctrl + Shift + IOpen browser dev tools (for troubleshooting)If the Falcon console is unresponsive
Speed Tip: When triaging a detection, right-click the SHA256 hash and select "Copy" — then paste directly into the CrowdStrike Intelligence search bar or VirusTotal. Saves typing and avoids transcription errors on 64-character hashes. Harris Health System — InfoSec Confidential | CrowdStrike Falcon SOC L1 SOP v1.0 | Last updated: March 2026 | Questions? It covers ~13,000 IT endpoints across 2 hospitals and 32 clinics. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint runs in passive mode alongside Falcon (for Purview telemetry only). CrowdStrike Overwatch provides 24/7 human-led threat hunting on top of automated detections. If you see a Defender alert, it's informational — CrowdStrike is the blocking/containment tool. A 6-9 month parallel evaluation is underway to make a data-driven decision on whether to keep both. 🖥️ Console Navigation Use this section to find your way around the Falcon console. The console is accessed via falcon.crowdstrike.com (https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) using Harris Health SSO. Key Areas You'll Use Daily 1 Activity Dashboard (Home) Where: Landing page after login, or left menu → Activity → Detections What it shows: All active detections across the environment, sorted by severity and time. This is your primary alert queue for CrowdStrike alerts. L1 Action: Start here every shift. Filter by severity (Critical/High first), then review unassigned detections. 2 Host Management Where: Left menu → Host setup and management → Host Management What it shows: All enrolled endpoints — hostname, IP, OS, sensor version, containment status, last seen time. L1 Action: Search for a specific host by hostname or IP. Check if a host is online, contained, or in Reduced Functionality Mode (RFM). This is where you initiate Network Containment . 3 Threat Intelligence → IOC Management Where: Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management What it shows: All custom IOCs (IPs, domains, file hashes) that have been added — either from the TIP automatically or manually by analysts. searchResults.snippet: Malware Analysis: Steps & Examples | CrowdStrike Cybersecurity 101: The Fundamentals of Cybersecurity > Malware Explained > Malware Analysis Explained Malware Analysis Explained Kurt Baker - March 04, 2025 What is malware analysis? Malware analysis is the process of understanding the behavior and purpose of a suspicious file or URL. The output of the analysis aids in the detection and mitigation of the potential threat. The key benefit of malware analysis is that it helps incident responders and security analysts: Pragmatically triage incidents by level of severity Uncover hidden indicators of compromise (IOCs) that should be blocked Improve the efficacy of IOC alerts and notifications Enrich context when threat hunting Types of malware analysis The analysis may be conducted in a manner that is static, dynamic or a hybrid of the two. Static analysis Basic static analysis does not require that the code is actually run. Instead, static analysis examines the file for signs of malicious intent. It can be useful to identify malicious infrastructure, libraries or packed files. Technical indicators are identified such as file names, hashes, strings such as IP addresses, domains, and file header data can be used to determine whether that file is malicious. In addition, tools like disassemblers and network analyzers can be used to observe the malware without actually running it in order to collect information on how the malware works. CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report AI threats have reached a critical turning point. Access the definitive look at the cyber threat landscape. Download However, since static analysis does not actually run the code, sophisticated malware can include malicious runtime behavior that can go undetected. For example, if a file generates a string that then downloads a malicious file based upon the dynamic string, it could go undetected by a basic static analysis. Enterprises have turned to dynamic analysis for a more complete understanding of the behavior of the file. Dynamic analysis Dynamic malware analysis executes suspected malicious code in a safe environment called a sandbox. This closed system enables security professionals to watch the malware in action without the risk of letting it infect their system or escape into the enterprise network. Dynamic analysis provides threat hunters and incident responders with deeper visibility, allowing them to uncover the true nature of a threat. As a secondary benefit, automated sandboxing eliminates the time it would take to reverse engineer a file to discover the malicious code. The challenge with dynamic analysis is that adversaries are smart, and they know sandboxes are out there, so they have become very good at detecting them. To deceive a sandbox, adversaries hide code inside them that may remain dormant until certain conditions are met. Only then does the code run. Hybrid analysis (includes both of the techniques above) Basic static analysis isn’t a reliable way to detect sophisticated malicious code, and sophisticated malware can sometimes hide from the presence of sandbox technology. By combining basic and dynamic analysis techniques, hybrid analysis provide security team the best of both approaches – primarily because it can detect malicious code that is trying to hide, and then can extract many more indicators of compromise (IOCs) by statically and previously unseen code. Hybrid analysis helps detect unknown threats, even those from the most sophisticated malware. For example, one of the things hybrid analysis does is apply static analysis to data generated by behavioral analysis – like when a piece of malicious code runs and generates some changes in memory. Dynamic analysis would detect that, and analysts would be alerted to circle back and perform basic static analysis on that memory dump. As a result, more IOCs would be generated and zero-day exploits would be exposed. Learn More Falcon Sandbox enables cybersecurity teams of all skill levels to increase their understanding of the threats they face and use that knowledge to defend against future attacks. Learn more about Falcon Sandbox here. Malware analysis use cases Malware detection Adversaries are employing more sophisticated techniques to avoid traditional detection mechanisms. By providing deep behavioral analysis and by identifying shared code, malicious functionality or infrastructure, threats can be more effectively detected. In addition, an output of malware analysis is the extraction of IOCs. The IOCs may then be fed into SEIMs, threat intelligence platforms (TIPs) and security orchestration tools to aid in alerting teams to related threats in the future. Threat alerts and triage Malware analysis solutions provide higher-fidelity alerts earlier in the attack life cycle. Therefore, teams can save time by prioritizing the results of these alerts over other technologies. Incident response The goal of the incident response (IR) team is to provide root cause analysis, determine impact and succeed in remediation and recovery. The malware analysis process aids in the efficiency and effectiveness of this effort. Threat hunting Malware analysis can expose behavior and artifacts that threat hunters can use to find similar activity, such as access to a particular network connection, port or domain. By searching firewall and proxy logs or SIEM data, teams can use this data to find similar threats. Malware research Academic or industry malware researchers perform malware analysis to gain an understanding of the latest techniques, exploits and tools used by adversaries. Stages of malware analysis Static properties analysis Static properties include strings embedded in the malware code, header details, hashes, metadata, embedded resources, etc. This type of data may be all that is needed to create IOCs, and they can be acquired very quickly because there is no need to run the program in order to see them. Insights gathered during the static properties analysis can indicate whether a deeper investigation using more comprehensive techniques is necessary and determine which steps should be taken next. Interactive behavior analysis Behavioral analysis is used to observe and interact with a malware sample running in a lab. Analysts seek to understand the sample’s registry, file system, process and network activities. They may also conduct memory forensics to learn how the malware uses memory. If the analysts suspect that the malware has a certain capability, they can set up a simulation to test their theory. Behavioral analysis requires a creative analyst with advanced skills. The process is time-consuming and complicated and cannot be performed effectively without automated tools. Fully automated analysis Fully automated analysis quickly and simply assesses suspicious files. The analysis can determine potential repercussions if the malware were to infiltrate the network and then produce an easy-to-read report that provides fast answers for security teams. Fully automated analysis is the best way to process malware at scale. Manual code reversing In this stage, analysts reverse-engineer code using debuggers, disassemblers, compilers and specialized tools to decode encrypted data, determine the logic behind the malware algorithm and understand any hidden capabilities that the malware has not yet exhibited. Code reversing is a rare skill, and executing code reversals takes a great deal of time. For these reasons, malware investigations often skip this step and therefore miss out on a lot of valuable insights into the nature of the malware. Learn More Learn about the largest online malware analysis community that is field-tested by tens of thousands of users every day. Download: Falcon Sandbox Malware Analysis Data Sheet. The world’s most powerful malware sandbox Security teams can use the CrowdStrike Falcon® Sandbox to understand sophisticated malware attacks and strengthen their defenses. Falcon Sandbox™ performs deep analyses of evasive and unknown threats, and enriches the results with threat intelligence. Key benefits of Falcon Sandbox Provides in-depth insight into all file, network and memory activity Offers leading anti-sandbox detection technology Generates intuitive reports with forensic data available on demand Supports the MITRE ATT&CK® framework Orchestrates workflows with an extensive application programming interface (API) and pre-built integrations Detect unknown threats Falcon Sandbox extracts more IOCs than any other competing sandbox solution by using a unique hybrid analysis technology to detect unknown and zero-day exploits. All data extracted from the hybrid analysis engine is processed automatically and integrated into Falcon Sandbox reports. Falcon Sandbox has anti-evasion technology that includes state-of-the-art anti-sandbox detection. File monitoring runs in the kernel and cannot be observed by user-mode applications. There is no agent that can be easily identified by malware, and each release is continuously tested to ensure Falcon Sandbox is nearly undetectable, even by malware using the most sophisticated sandbox detection techniques. The environment can be customized by date/time, environmental variables, user behaviors and more. Identify related threats Know how to defend against an attack by understanding the adversary. Falcon Sandbox provides insights into who is behind a malware attack through the use of malware search a unique capability that determines whether a malware file is related to a larger campaign, malware family or threat actor. Falcon Sandbox will automatically search the largest malware search engine in the cybersecurity industry to find related samples and, within seconds, expand the analysis to include all files. This is important because it provides analysts with a deeper understanding of the attack and a larger set of IOCs that can be used to better protect the organization. Achieve complete visibility Uncover the full attack life cycle with in-depth insight into all file, network, memory and process activity.…

Investigate Security Incidents in CrowdStrike: Threat Hunting & RTR Guide | Inventive HQ Home / Knowledge Base / CrowdStrike / Investigate Security Incidents in CrowdStrike: Threat Hunting & RTR Guide CrowdStrike advanced Investigate Security Incidents in CrowdStrike: Threat Hunting & RTR Guide Investigate security incidents using CrowdStrike Falcon with step-by-step detection analysis, Real-Time Response (RTR), threat hunting, and incident response. Learn to analyze detections, hunt threats, and contain compromised endpoints. 20 min read Updated January 2026 When a security incident occurs, CrowdStrike Falcon provides detailed threat intelligence, behavioral analysis, and forensic data to help administrators investigate and respond quickly. This guide explains how to analyze security alerts, trace attack patterns, and take appropriate remediation steps using the Falcon Console. This is not a comprehensive guide Step 1: Log Into the Falcon Console Open a browser and go to: https://falcon.crowdstrike.com or https://falcon.us-2.crowdstrike.com/ (Varies by tenant). Sign in using your admin credentials. Step 2: Review Detections and Alerts View Active Detections In the left-hand menu, navigate to Activity > Detections. Filter results by: Severity (Low, Medium, High, Critical) Timeframe (Last 24 hours, Last 7 days, Custom) Host Name (Investigate a specific endpoint) Analyze a Detection Alert Click on a detection to open detailed information. Review the MITRE ATT&CK Tactics & Techniques used in the attack. Check the Process Tree to see how the attack progressed. Look at the Threat Graph to understand relationships between processes.📌 Example: If a suspicious PowerShell script was executed, check which parent process initiated it and what actions were performed. Step 3: Investigate the Affected Endpoint View Host Details Navigate to Hosts > Host Management. Search for the affected host by Hostname or IP Address. Review: Sensor Health Status (Ensure the Falcon Sensor is running). Recent Activities (Processes, network connections, registry changes). Quarantine Status (Check if the system is isolated). Perform a Live Investigation Using Real Time Response (RTR) Click on the affected host and select Real Time Response (RTR). Run diagnostic commands to gather more details: List running processes: ps 
Copy Check network connections: netstat -ano 
Copy Review scheduled tasks: schtasks /query /fo LIST 
Copy Identify suspicious activity and determine the scope of the attack. Step 4: Contain and Remediate the Threat Option 1: Quarantine the Host In Host Management, select the affected endpoint. Click Contain Host to isolate it from the network. This prevents further spread while investigation continues. Option 2: Kill Malicious Processes In Real Time Response (RTR), run the following command (Replace with the process ID of the malicious activity):- kill  Option 3: Delete a Malicious File Run the following command in RTR: del "C:\\path\\to\\ malware.exe"  This removes the malware from the system. Step 5: Check for Additional Compromise Indicators Hunt for Related Threats Navigate to Threat Intelligence > IOCs. Search for file hashes, domains, or IP addresses linked to the attack. Cross-check detections to see if other endpoints were affected. Review Falcon OverWatch Threat Reports Go to OverWatch > Threat Hunting Reports. Look for campaign-based attacks and persistent threats. Take action on any additional endpoints showing suspicious activity. Step 6: Apply Prevention Measures Update Prevention Policies Go to Configuration > Prevention Policies. Tighten existing rules: Increase machine learning sensitivity. Restrict PowerShell and script execution. Enable USB Device Control if applicable. Block Malicious IOCs Navigate to Threat Intelligence > Indicators of Compromise (IOCs). Click Add IOC and enter known malicious file hashes, domains, or IPs. Set action to Detect & Prevent to block future attacks. Patch Vulnerabilities Use Falcon Spotlight to check for missing security patches. Apply necessary OS and software updates across all endpoints. Best Practices for Security Incident Investigation ✅ Act Quickly – Investigate high-severity detections immediately. ✅ Use Real-Time Response (RTR) – Gain deeper visibility into compromised hosts. ✅ Contain First, Investigate Second – Prevent further damage by isolating threats. ✅ Enable Threat Hunting – Use Falcon OverWatch for proactive security monitoring. Frequently Asked Questions Find answers to common questions What are the best practices for using Real-Time Response (RTR) in CrowdStrike Falcon during an active security incident? To effectively use Real-Time Response (RTR) during a security incident, ensure you have admin-level access in the Falcon Console. Start by listing running processes with 'ps' to identify suspicious activity, such as unusual parent-child relationships. Check network connections using 'netstat -ano' to detect unauthorized outbound communications, which may indicate command and control (C2) activity. Document your findings for reporting and future reference. Establish a baseline of normal activity to differentiate between false positives and real threats. If you find a malicious process, use the 'kill' command with the process ID (PID) to terminate it, but avoid killing system-critical processes. After isolating the affected host with the 'Contain Host' feature, review other endpoints for similar behaviors using Threat Intelligence IOCs. How can I effectively correlate findings from multiple endpoints during a security incident investigation in CrowdStrike Falcon? To correlate findings across multiple endpoints in CrowdStrike Falcon during a security incident, utilize the Threat Intelligence and IOC features. Start by accessing 'Threat Intelligence' to cross-reference IOCs, such as malicious file hashes or IP addresses, to identify affected endpoints. Then, go to 'Activity > Detections' and filter alerts by severity and timeframe to prioritize investigations. Look for patterns, such as multiple endpoints executing the same PowerShell script, indicating a coordinated attack. Implement a real-time incident response team strategy to discuss findings, and leverage Falcon OverWatch Threat Reports for insights into ongoing campaigns. Finally, consolidate your findings into a post-incident report to enhance future detection and response strategies. What steps should I take to tighten prevention policies in CrowdStrike Falcon after a security incident has been identified? After a security incident, tighten your CrowdStrike Falcon prevention policies by following these steps: Go to 'Configuration > Prevention Policies' and increase machine learning sensitivity to 'high' to enhance detection, accepting potential false positives. Restrict PowerShell and script execution by whitelisting approved scripts to reduce attack surfaces. Enable USB Device Control if removable media is unnecessary to prevent data exfiltration. In 'Threat Intelligence > Indicators of Compromise (IOCs)', add known malicious file hashes, IPs, or domains and set their action to 'Detect & Prevent'. Use Falcon Spotlight to identify and patch any missing updates on endpoints, addressing vulnerabilities that could be exploited in future attacks. These measures will enhance your security posture and mitigate future risks. Need help shipping something? Pro Tip: Bookmark these direct URLs for fastest access: • Detections: falcon.crowdstrike.com/activity-v2/detections • Host Management: falcon.crowdstrike.com/hosts/hosts • IOC Management: falcon.crowdstrike.com/iocs/indicators • Event Search: falcon.crowdstrike.com/investigate/events 🔔 Reading a Detection Use this section to understand what every field in a CrowdStrike detection means. When a detection fires, the alert card contains all the information you need to start your triage. Anatomy of a Falcon Detection Alert
FieldWhat It MeansL1 Action
Detection NameShort description of what was detected (e.g., "Malware Detection," "Intel Detection," "Suspicious Process"). See the Detection Names cheat sheet for common ones.Tells you the category — is this malware, behavioral, intelligence-based, or policy violation?
SeverityInformational, Low, Medium, High, or Critical. Based on CrowdStrike's assessment of threat level. See the Severity → SLA cheat sheet.Determines your response urgency and SLA. Critical/High = immediate triage.
Tactic & TechniqueMITRE ATT&CK tactic (e.g., Execution, Persistence, Lateral Movement) and technique (e.g., T1059 Command-Line Interface). See the MITRE cheat sheet.Tells you what the attacker is trying to do. Lateral Movement or Defense Evasion = escalate quickly.
HostnameThe name of the endpoint where the detection occurred.Look this up in SolarWinds to find the asset owner and classification.
UsernameThe logged-in user account at the time of the detection.Check if this is an admin, clinical user (Imprivata), or standard user. Admin accounts = higher severity.
File Name / PathThe executable or script that triggered the detection.Is this a known IT tool or a suspicious file? Check the file path — C:\Users\.\Downloads is riskier than C:\Windows\System32.
Command LineThe full command-line arguments used to launch the process.This is critical — encoded PowerShell, -ExecutionPolicy Bypass, or net use \ are red flags. Copy this for your notes.
SHA256 HashUnique fingerprint of the file. Used for IOC lookups.Copy this and look it up in CrowdStrike Intelligence + VirusTotal.
Parent ProcessThe process that launched the detected process.Key for process tree analysis — see the next section.
Detection ActionWhat Falcon did: Blocked (prevented execution), Detected (alerted but allowed), or Quarantined (isolated the file).If Detected only (not blocked), the threat may still be active — higher urgency.
Network AccessesAny network connections the process made — IPs, ports, domains.Check destination IPs/domains against threat intel. Outbound to external IP on port 443/80/8080 from a suspicious process = possible C2.
Overwatch Detections: If the detection source is Falcon Overwatch (not automated rules), a human CrowdStrike threat hunter identified this activity. Treat as HIGH confidence and escalate to L2 immediately regardless of the stated severity. 🌳 Investigating a Process Tree The process tree is the most powerful investigation tool in CrowdStrike. It shows you the parent-child relationship of every process — who launched what, in what order, and with what arguments. Learning to read it is the single most important L1 skill. How to Access the Process Tree 1 Open the detection From Activity → Detections , click on the detection alert to open the detail view. 2 Expand the Process Tree Click "View Full Detection"

or the process tree icon. The tree shows all processes in the execution chain — the detected process is highlighted in red. 3 Read top-down: Parent → Child The topmost process is the root (usually explorer.exe for user-initiated or services.exe for system-initiated). Each indented level below is a child process launched by its parent. Example: Normal vs. Suspicious Process Trees Normal — User opens a Word document: explorer.exe (user desktop shell) └─ WINWORD.EXE (Microsoft Word) └─ splwow64.exe (print spooler — normal for Word) Suspicious — Word document drops a payload: explorer.exe └─ WINWORD.EXE └─ cmd.exe /c powershell -enc SQBFAFgA. ← ⚠️ Word spawning cmd/PowerShell = malicious └─ powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass ← ⚠️ Encoded command + bypass └─ whoami.exe ← ⚠️ Reconnaissance └─ net.exe user /domain ← ⚠️ AD enumeration Suspicious — Lateral movement via PsExec: services.exe (system) └─ PSEXESVC.exe ← ⚠️ PsExec service installed remotely └─ cmd.exe └─ net.exe use \DC01\C$ /user:admin ← ⚠️ Mapping admin shares └─ vssadmin.exe delete shadows /all /quiet ← 🔴 RANSOMWARE PRECURSOR Red Flags to Look For in Process Trees
PatternWhat It MeansSeverity
Office app (Word, Excel, Outlook) → cmd.exe or powershell.exeMacro-based malware — the document is running commandsHIGH
powershell.exe -enc or -EncodedCommandBase64-encoded PowerShell — attacker hiding their commandsHIGH
cmd.exe /c followed by whoami, ipconfig, net userReconnaissance — attacker mapping the environmentMEDIUM
rundll32.exe loading an unusual DLLLiving-off-the-land (LOLBin) — abusing legitimate Windows toolsHIGH
mshta.exe, wscript.exe, cscript.exe from temp folderScript-based malware executionHIGH
certutil.exe -urlcache or bitsadmin /transferDownloading payload using built-in Windows toolsCRITICAL
schtasks /create or sc createEstablishing persistence — attacker wants to survive rebootHIGH
vssadmin delete shadowsDeleting shadow copies — imminent ransomware encryptionCRITICAL
PsExec, wmic /node:, schtasks /s Lateral movement — attacker spreading to other hostsCRITICAL
mimikatz or lsass.exe access by non-system processCredential dumping — attacker stealing passwords/hashesCRITICAL
LOLBins (Living-off-the-Land Binaries) are legitimate Windows tools that attackers abuse to avoid detection. The most common ones: powershell.exe , cmd.exe , rundll32.exe , mshta.exe , certutil.exe , bitsadmin.exe , regsvr32.exe , wscript.exe , cscript.exe , msiexec.exe . These are not malicious by themselves — it's the context (parent process, command-line args, file path) that makes them suspicious. 🔒 Host Containment & Lift Network containment isolates a compromised endpoint from the network while keeping its connection to the CrowdStrike cloud. The host loses all network access except CrowdStrike management and any whitelisted IPs. Use this when you need to stop lateral movement or data exfiltration immediately. How to Contain a Host 1 Navigate to the host Go to Host setup and management → Host Management . Search for the hostname or IP. Click the host name to open its details. 2 Click Actions → Contain Host On the host detail page, click Actions in the top-right corner. Select "Contain" from the dropdown menu. 3 Confirm containment Click Confirm . Containment should complete within seconds. The host status will change to "Contained"

. If it shows "Containment Pending," the endpoint may be offline. 4 Verify containment Refresh the host page. Confirm status shows Contained . The host can still communicate with CrowdStrike cloud (so you can still run RTR commands and pull data) but cannot reach anything else on the network. Before containing a medical device: NEVER contain an endpoint on a medical device VLAN without Clinical Engineering approval. Medical devices may be actively involved in patient care. Use Cisco FMC microsegmentation as a compensating control instead. See the Medical Device playbook in the Triage Decision Tree. How to Lift Containment 1 Navigate to the contained host In Host Management , filter by "Contained" status at the top. Click the host name. 2 Click "Lift Containment" Click the "Status: Contained" button. Add a comment explaining why containment is being lifted (e.g., "Reimaged and verified clean by L2 — ticket INC0012345"). Click Confirm . 3 Verify normal status Refresh the page. Status should change from "Lift Containment Pending" to "Normal" . The host regains full network access. L1 Authority: L1 analysts CAN contain a host without prior approval during an active threat (malware executing, C2 callback, ransomware indicators). Document your reasoning in the alert notes and ServiceNow ticket. L1 analysts should get L2 approval before lifting containment to ensure remediation is complete. 🎯 IOC Management Use this section to add, search, and manage custom Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) in CrowdStrike Falcon. IOCs are pushed here from the TIP (Sentinel) automatically, but you may also need to add them manually from threat advisories. How to Add a Custom IOC 1 Navigate to IOC Management Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management . You'll see the list of all existing IOCs. 2 Click "Add IOC" Select the IOC type: File Hash (SHA256 preferred, also SHA1 or MD5), Domain , or IP Address . 3 Enter the IOC value For hashes, paste the full SHA256. For domains, enter without protocol (e.g., evil-domain.com , not https://evil-domain.com ). For IPs, enter the IPv4 address. 4 Set the action Detect — alerts only, does not block. Use when you want to monitor. Detect & Prevent — blocks execution/connection AND alerts. Use for confirmed malicious IOCs and active exploitation. 5 Set severity and description Match the advisory severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low). In the description, include the source: e.g., "Health-ISAC Advisory 2026-042" or "CISA KEV — CVE-2026-12345". 6 Set expiration Harris Health policy: no permanent IOCs . Set 30 days for actively exploited threats (renew if still active). Set 90 days for general threat intel. IOCs auto-expire and are cleaned up. 7 Save and verify Click Save . Verify the IOC appears in the IOC Management list. Monitor the Detection Summary for any hits. Searching for existing IOCs: Use the search bar at the top of IOC Management. Paste a hash, IP, or domain to check if it's already in the system before adding a duplicate. 🔍 Hash & IOC Lookup When you encounter an unknown file hash, IP, or domain during an investigation, use these tools to determine if it's malicious. Lookup Priority Order 1 CrowdStrike Intelligence (first) Where: Threat Intelligence → Intelligence → paste the hash, IP, or domain in the search bar. Why first: CrowdStrike's intel database includes healthcare-specific threat data and correlates with adversary campaigns. It's the most contextually relevant source for Harris Health. What to look for: Malware family, associated adversary group, kill chain phase, severity rating, and any related IOCs. 2 VirusTotal (second) Where: virustotal.com (https://www.virustotal.com) → paste the hash, IP, or domain. Why second: VirusTotal aggregates results from 70+ antivirus engines. Good for confirmation and broad coverage, but lacks the adversary context that CrowdStrike provides. What to look for: Detection ratio (e.g., 45/70 engines detect = confirmed malicious). Community comments. Behavioral analysis results if the file was sandboxed. OPSEC Warning: Do NOT upload actual files from Harris Health endpoints to VirusTotal. VirusTotal is a public service — uploaded files become visible to other researchers and potentially adversaries. Only submit hashes (SHA256) for lookup, never the actual file. If you need to detonate a file in a sandbox, use CrowdStrike's sandbox or coordinate with L2. 💻 Real-Time Response (RTR) Real-Time Response gives you a remote shell to a live endpoint. Use it for live forensics — pulling files, checking running processes, killing malicious processes. The endpoint must be online and have an active CrowdStrike sensor. Caution:

Malware Analysis: Steps & Examples | CrowdStrike Cybersecurity 101: The Fundamentals of Cybersecurity > Malware Explained > Malware Analysis Explained Malware Analysis Explained Kurt Baker - March 04, 2025 What is malware analysis? Malware analysis is the process of understanding the behavior and purpose of a suspicious file or URL. The output of the analysis aids in the detection and mitigation of the potential threat. The key benefit of malware analysis is that it helps incident responders and security analysts: Pragmatically triage incidents by level of severity Uncover hidden indicators of compromise (IOCs) that should be blocked Improve the efficacy of IOC alerts and notifications Enrich context when threat hunting Types of malware analysis The analysis may be conducted in a manner that is static, dynamic or a hybrid of the two. Static analysis Basic static analysis does not require that the code is actually run. Instead, static analysis examines the file for signs of malicious intent. It can be useful to identify malicious infrastructure, libraries or packed files. Technical indicators are identified such as file names, hashes, strings such as IP addresses, domains, and file header data can be used to determine whether that file is malicious. In addition, tools like disassemblers and network analyzers can be used to observe the malware without actually running it in order to collect information on how the malware works. CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report AI threats have reached a critical turning point. Access the definitive look at the cyber threat landscape. Download However, since static analysis does not actually run the code, sophisticated malware can include malicious runtime behavior that can go undetected. For example, if a file generates a string that then downloads a malicious file based upon the dynamic string, it could go undetected by a basic static analysis. Enterprises have turned to dynamic analysis for a more complete understanding of the behavior of the file. Dynamic analysis Dynamic malware analysis executes suspected malicious code in a safe environment called a sandbox. This closed system enables security professionals to watch the malware in action without the risk of letting it infect their system or escape into the enterprise network. Dynamic analysis provides threat hunters and incident responders with deeper visibility, allowing them to uncover the true nature of a threat. As a secondary benefit, automated sandboxing eliminates the time it would take to reverse engineer a file to discover the malicious code. The challenge with dynamic analysis is that adversaries are smart, and they know sandboxes are out there, so they have become very good at detecting them. To deceive a sandbox, adversaries hide code inside them that may remain dormant until certain conditions are met. Only then does the code run. Hybrid analysis (includes both of the techniques above) Basic static analysis isn’t a reliable way to detect sophisticated malicious code, and sophisticated malware can sometimes hide from the presence of sandbox technology. By combining basic and dynamic analysis techniques, hybrid analysis provide security team the best of both approaches – primarily because it can detect malicious code that is trying to hide, and then can extract many more indicators of compromise (IOCs) by statically and previously unseen code. Hybrid analysis helps detect unknown threats, even those from the most sophisticated malware. For example, one of the things hybrid analysis does is apply static analysis to data generated by behavioral analysis – like when a piece of malicious code runs and generates some changes in memory. Dynamic analysis would detect that, and analysts would be alerted to circle back and perform basic static analysis on that memory dump. As a result, more IOCs would be generated and zero-day exploits would be exposed. Learn More Falcon Sandbox enables cybersecurity teams of all skill levels to increase their understanding of the threats they face and use that knowledge to defend against future attacks. Learn more about Falcon Sandbox here. Malware analysis use cases Malware detection Adversaries are employing more sophisticated techniques to avoid traditional detection mechanisms. By providing deep behavioral analysis and by identifying shared code, malicious functionality or infrastructure, threats can be more effectively detected. In addition, an output of malware analysis is the extraction of IOCs. The IOCs may then be fed into SEIMs, threat intelligence platforms (TIPs) and security orchestration tools to aid in alerting teams to related threats in the future. Threat alerts and triage Malware analysis solutions provide higher-fidelity alerts earlier in the attack life cycle. Therefore, teams can save time by prioritizing the results of these alerts over other technologies. Incident response The goal of the incident response (IR) team is to provide root cause analysis, determine impact and succeed in remediation and recovery. The malware analysis process aids in the efficiency and effectiveness of this effort. Threat hunting Malware analysis can expose behavior and artifacts that threat hunters can use to find similar activity, such as access to a particular network connection, port or domain. By searching firewall and proxy logs or SIEM data, teams can use this data to find similar threats. Malware research Academic or industry malware researchers perform malware analysis to gain an understanding of the latest techniques, exploits and tools used by adversaries. Stages of malware analysis Static properties analysis Static properties include strings embedded in the malware code, header details, hashes, metadata, embedded resources, etc. This type of data may be all that is needed to create IOCs, and they can be acquired very quickly because there is no need to run the program in order to see them. Insights gathered during the static properties analysis can indicate whether a deeper investigation using more comprehensive techniques is necessary and determine which steps should be taken next. Interactive behavior analysis Behavioral analysis is used to observe and interact with a malware sample running in a lab. Analysts seek to understand the sample’s registry, file system, process and network activities. They may also conduct memory forensics to learn how the malware uses memory. If the analysts suspect that the malware has a certain capability, they can set up a simulation to test their theory. Behavioral analysis requires a creative analyst with advanced skills. The process is time-consuming and complicated and cannot be performed effectively without automated tools. Fully automated analysis Fully automated analysis quickly and simply assesses suspicious files. The analysis can determine potential repercussions if the malware were to infiltrate the network and then produce an easy-to-read report that provides fast answers for security teams. Fully automated analysis is the best way to process malware at scale. Manual code reversing In this stage, analysts reverse-engineer code using debuggers, disassemblers, compilers and specialized tools to decode encrypted data, determine the logic behind the malware algorithm and understand any hidden capabilities that the malware has not yet exhibited. Code reversing is a rare skill, and executing code reversals takes a great deal of time. For these reasons, malware investigations often skip this step and therefore miss out on a lot of valuable insights into the nature of the malware. or the process tree icon. The tree shows all processes in the execution chain — the detected process is highlighted in red. 3 Read top-down: Parent → Child The topmost process is the root (usually explorer.exe for user-initiated or services.exe for system-initiated). Each indented level below is a child process launched by its parent. Example: Normal vs. Suspicious Process Trees Normal — User opens a Word document: explorer.exe (user desktop shell) └─ WINWORD.EXE (Microsoft Word) └─ splwow64.exe (print spooler — normal for Word) Suspicious — Word document drops a payload: explorer.exe └─ WINWORD.EXE └─ cmd.exe /c powershell -enc SQBFAFgA. ← ⚠️ Word spawning cmd/PowerShell = malicious └─ powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass ← ⚠️ Encoded command + bypass └─ whoami.exe ← ⚠️ Reconnaissance └─ net.exe user /domain ← ⚠️ AD enumeration Suspicious — Lateral movement via PsExec: services.exe (system) └─ PSEXESVC.exe ← ⚠️ PsExec service installed remotely └─ cmd.exe └─ net.exe use \DC01\C$ /user:admin ← ⚠️ Mapping admin shares └─ vssadmin.exe delete shadows /all /quiet ← 🔴 RANSOMWARE PRECURSOR Red Flags to Look For in Process Trees
PatternWhat It MeansSeverity
Office app (Word, Excel, Outlook) → cmd.exe or powershell.exeMacro-based malware — the document is running commandsHIGH
powershell.exe -enc or -EncodedCommandBase64-encoded PowerShell — attacker hiding their commandsHIGH
cmd.exe /c followed by whoami, ipconfig, net userReconnaissance — attacker mapping the environmentMEDIUM
rundll32.exe loading an unusual DLLLiving-off-the-land (LOLBin) — abusing legitimate Windows toolsHIGH
mshta.exe, wscript.exe, cscript.exe from temp folderScript-based malware executionHIGH
certutil.exe -urlcache or bitsadmin /transferDownloading payload using built-in Windows toolsCRITICAL
schtasks /create or sc createEstablishing persistence — attacker wants to survive rebootHIGH
vssadmin delete shadowsDeleting shadow copies — imminent ransomware encryptionCRITICAL
PsExec, wmic /node:, schtasks /s Lateral movement — attacker spreading to other hostsCRITICAL
mimikatz or lsass.exe access by non-system processCredential dumping — attacker stealing passwords/hashesCRITICAL
LOLBins (Living-off-the-Land Binaries) are legitimate Windows tools that attackers abuse to avoid detection. The most common ones: powershell.exe , cmd.exe , rundll32.exe , mshta.exe , certutil.exe , bitsadmin.exe , regsvr32.exe , wscript.exe , cscript.exe , msiexec.exe . These are not malicious by themselves — it's the context (parent process, command-line args, file path) that makes them suspicious. 🔒 Host Containment & Lift Network containment isolates a compromised endpoint from the network while keeping its connection to the CrowdStrike cloud. The host loses all network access except CrowdStrike management and any whitelisted IPs. Use this when you need to stop lateral movement or data exfiltration immediately. How to Contain a Host 1 Navigate to the host Go to Host setup and management → Host Management . Search for the hostname or IP. Click the host name to open its details. 2 Click Actions → Contain Host On the host detail page, click Actions in the top-right corner. Select "Contain" from the dropdown menu. 3 Confirm containment Click Confirm . Containment should complete within seconds. The host status will change to "Contained"

For these reasons, malware investigations often skip this step and therefore miss out on a lot of valuable insights into the nature of the malware. Learn More Learn about the largest online malware analysis community that is field-tested by tens of thousands of users every day. Download: Falcon Sandbox Malware Analysis Data Sheet. The world’s most powerful malware sandbox Security teams can use the CrowdStrike Falcon® Sandbox to understand sophisticated malware attacks and strengthen their defenses.
