The tree shows all processes in the execution chain — the detected process is highlighted in red. 3 Read top-down: Parent → Child The topmost process is the root (usually explorer.exe for user-initiated or services.exe for system-initiated). Each indented level below is a child process launched by its parent. Example: Normal vs. Suspicious Process Trees Normal — User opens a Word document: explorer.exe (user desktop shell) └─ WINWORD.EXE (Microsoft Word) └─ splwow64.exe (print spooler — normal for Word) Suspicious — Word document drops a payload: explorer.exe └─ WINWORD.EXE └─ cmd.exe /c powershell -enc SQBFAFgA. ← ⚠️ Word spawning cmd/PowerShell = malicious └─ powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass ← ⚠️ Encoded command + bypass └─ whoami.exe ← ⚠️ Reconnaissance └─ net.exe user /domain ← ⚠️ AD enumeration Suspicious — Lateral movement via PsExec: services.exe (system) └─ PSEXESVC.exe ← ⚠️ PsExec service installed remotely └─ cmd.exe └─ net.exe use \DC01\C$ /user:admin ← ⚠️ Mapping admin shares └─ vssadmin.exe delete shadows /all /quiet ← 🔴 RANSOMWARE PRECURSOR Red Flags to Look For in Process Trees
PatternWhat It MeansSeverity
Office app (Word, Excel, Outlook) → cmd.exe or powershell.exeMacro-based malware — the document is running commandsHIGH
powershell.exe -enc or -EncodedCommandBase64-encoded PowerShell — attacker hiding their commandsHIGH
cmd.exe /c followed by whoami, ipconfig, net userReconnaissance — attacker mapping the environmentMEDIUM
rundll32.exe loading an unusual DLLLiving-off-the-land (LOLBin) — abusing legitimate Windows toolsHIGH
mshta.exe, wscript.exe, cscript.exe from temp folderScript-based malware executionHIGH
certutil.exe -urlcache or bitsadmin /transferDownloading payload using built-in Windows toolsCRITICAL
schtasks /create or sc createEstablishing persistence — attacker wants to survive rebootHIGH
vssadmin delete shadowsDeleting shadow copies — imminent ransomware encryptionCRITICAL
PsExec, wmic /node:, schtasks /s Lateral movement — attacker spreading to other hostsCRITICAL
mimikatz or lsass.exe access by non-system processCredential dumping — attacker stealing passwords/hashesCRITICAL
LOLBins (Living-off-the-Land Binaries) are legitimate Windows tools that attackers abuse to avoid detection. The most common ones: powershell.exe , cmd.exe , rundll32.exe , mshta.exe , certutil.exe , bitsadmin.exe , regsvr32.exe , wscript.exe , cscript.exe , msiexec.exe . These are not malicious by themselves — it's the context (parent process, command-line args, file path) that makes them suspicious. 🔒 Host Containment & Lift Network containment isolates a compromised endpoint from the network while keeping its connection to the CrowdStrike cloud. The host loses all network access except CrowdStrike management and any whitelisted IPs. Use this when you need to stop lateral movement or data exfiltration immediately. How to Contain a Host 1 Navigate to the host Go to Host setup and management → Host Management . Search for the hostname or IP. Click the host name to open its details. 2 Click Actions → Contain Host On the host detail page, click Actions in the top-right corner. Select "Contain" from the dropdown menu. 3 Confirm containment Click Confirm . Containment should complete within seconds. The host status will change to "Contained"

activities.text:
Great question! Before diving in, a couple of clarifying points will help you triage more effectively:

- **What is the CrowdStrike alert severity?** (Critical, High, Medium, Low)
- **Is the affected system a medical device, domain controller, PHI system, or internet-facing server?**

That said, here is the full malware triage procedure per the **Harris Health CrowdStrike Falcon SOP**:

---

## 🧭 Summary
A user reporting malware issues should be triaged via the **CrowdStrike Falcon console**. Your goal is to assess severity, investigate the process tree, contain the host if necessary, and escalate appropriately.

---

## 🔢 Steps

### Step 1 — Log Into the Falcon Console
Log into [falcon.crowdstrike.com](https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) using your Harris Health SSO credentials.[1]

Navigate to:
**Activity Dashboard** → Left menu → **Activity → Detections**. This is your primary alert queue. Filter by severity — **Critical/High first** — then review unassigned detections.[1]

---

### Step 2 — Read the Detection Alert
Review each of the following fields on the alert card:


| Field | What to Check |
|---|---|
| **Detection Name** | Is this malware, behavioral, or intel-based? |
| **Severity** | Determines urgency and SLA |
| **Tactic & Technique** | MITRE ATT&CK — Lateral Movement or Defense Evasion = escalate quickly |
| **Hostname** | Look up in SolarWinds for asset owner and classification |
| **Username** | Is this an admin, clinical (Imprivata), or standard user? Admin = higher severity |
| **File Name / Path** | `C:\Users\...\Downloads` is riskier than `C:\Windows\System32` |
| **Command Line** | Encoded PowerShell, `-ExecutionPolicy Bypass`, or `net use \\` = red flags |
| **SHA256 Hash** | Copy and look up in CrowdStrike Intelligence + VirusTotal |
| **Detection Action** | If only **Detected** (not Blocked), threat may still be active — higher urgency |
| **Network Accesses** | Outbound to external IP on port 443/80/8080 from suspicious process = possible C2 |
[2]

> ⚠️ **Overwatch Detections**: If the detection source is **Falcon Overwatch**, a human CrowdStrike threat hunter identified this activity. Treat as **HIGH confidence** and escalate to L2 immediately regardless of stated severity.[2]

---

### Step 3 — Check Severity SLA


| Severity | Response | Acknowledge Within | Escalate To |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Critical** | Immediate containment. Phone L2 + SOC Manager. CISO notification for ransomware/breach | < 5 min | L2 + SOC Mgr + CISO |
| **High** | Immediate triage. Contain if active. Always escalate to L2 | < 10 min | L2 |
| **Medium** | Triage within shift. Investigate process tree and context. Escalate if unsure | < 30 min | L2 if unsure |
| **Low** | Triage when queue permits. Check against known FP list. Document and close | < 2 hours | No escalation (unless pattern of multiple Lows on one host) |
[3]

> 🔴 **Override Rule**: If a CrowdStrike detection involves a **PHI system**, **domain controller**, **internet-facing server**, or **medical device**, add **+1 severity level** to the response. A "Medium" on a domain controller is treated as High.[3]

---

### Step 4 — Investigate the Process Tree

From **Activity → Detections**, click the detection alert, then click **"View Full Detection"** or the process tree icon. The tree shows all processes in the execution chain — the detected process is highlighted in red.[2]

**Key red flags to look for:**


| Pattern | Meaning | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| Office app → `cmd.exe` or `powershell.exe` | Macro-based malware | HIGH |
| `powershell.exe -enc` or `-EncodedCommand` | Attacker hiding commands | HIGH |
| `certutil.exe -urlcache` or `bitsadmin /transfer` | Downloading payload with built-in tools | CRITICAL |
| `vssadmin delete shadows` | Imminent ransomware encryption | CRITICAL |
| `PsExec`, `wmic /node:` | Lateral movement | CRITICAL |
| `mimikatz` or `lsass.exe` access | Credential dumping | CRITICAL |
[4]

---

### Step 5 — Look Up File Hash / IOCs

**Lookup Priority:**
1. **CrowdStrike Intelligence first** — Go to *Threat Intelligence → Intelligence*, paste the SHA256 hash. Look for: malware family, adversary group, kill chain phase.
2. **VirusTotal second** — Go to [virustotal.com](https://www.virustotal.com), paste the hash. Look for detection ratio and community comments.

> ⚠️ **OPSEC Warning**: Do **NOT** upload actual files from Harris Health endpoints to VirusTotal. Only submit **hashes (SHA256)**. If you need to sandbox a file, use CrowdStrike's sandbox or coordinate with L2.[5]

---

### Step 6 — Contain the Host (If Active Threat)

If malware is actively executing, performing C2 callbacks, or showing ransomware indicators:


1. Go to **Host Management** → search for the hostname or IP → click the host name.
2. Click **Actions → Contain Host** → select "Contain" → click **Confirm**.
3. Refresh the host page and confirm status shows **"Contained"**.
[4]The host can still communicate with the CrowdStrike cloud (so you can still run RTR commands and pull data) but cannot reach anything else on the network.[5]

> 🏥 **NEVER contain an endpoint on a medical device VLAN without Clinical Engineering approval.** Medical devices may be actively involved in patient care. Use Cisco FMC microsegmentation as a compensating control instead.[5]

> ✅ **L1 Authority**: L1 analysts **CAN** contain a host without prior approval during an active threat (malware executing, C2 callback, ransomware indicators). Document your reasoning in the alert notes and ServiceNow ticket. L1 analysts should get **L2 approval before lifting containment** to ensure remediation is complete.[5]

---

## 🔼 Escalate If…
- Severity is **Critical or High** → escalate to L2 immediately
- You see **ransomware indicators** (e.g., `vssadmin delete shadows`) → escalate to L2 + SOC Manager + CISO
- The affected system is a **domain controller, PHI system, or medical device**
- Detection source is **Falcon Overwatch**
- You are unsure on a **Medium** detection

---

## 📋 Artifacts to Capture
- Alert name, severity, timestamp
- Hostname and IP
- Username (admin vs. standard)
- SHA256 hash
- Command-line arguments
- CrowdStrike Intelligence and VirusTotal results
- ServiceNow ticket number
- Containment action (if taken) with justification

---

Do you have the severity level or system type for this specific alert? That will help me give you more targeted guidance!
